title: AI Concepts in Pure Python
description: Fundamental concepts used in AI/ML, implemented without external libraries to understand the math.

tutorial.title: What is a Dot Product?
tutorial.content:
The dot product is a way to multiply two lists of numbers (vectors) together.
It's crucial in AI because it represents how similar two vectors are, or
the strength of a signal passing through a neuron.

Formula:  A · B = A[0]*B[0] + A[1]*B[1] + ... + A[n-1]*B[n-1]

tutorial.title: Dot Product Calculation
Why It Matters in AI
tutorial.content:
In a neural network:
  • Inputs   → your data (e.g., pixel brightness values)
  • Weights  → what the AI has learned from training
  • Dot product = weighted sum = the neuron's 'decision'

Higher dot product = the input is more aligned with what the
neuron has learned to look for.

tutorial.title: Real-World Analogy
tutorial.content:
Imagine you're rating a movie with 3 criteria:
  → Story quality:   score = 8
  → Acting quality:  score = 6
  → Visual effects:  score = 9

Your personal weights for these criteria:
  → Story:   weight = 0.6  (you care a lot)
  → Acting:  weight = 0.3
  → Visuals: weight = 0.1  (you care least)

Overall rating = dot([8,6,9], [0.6,0.3,0.1]) = 7.1

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: What is the dot product of [1, 2] and [3, 4]?

tutorial.title: Measuring Error
tutorial.content:
How do we know if an AI model is wrong? We measure the 'loss' or 'error'.
MSE calculates the average squared difference between what the model
predicted and what the actual answer was.

tutorial.title: Why SQUARE the error?
tutorial.content:
Squaring the error has two key benefits:

  1. Makes all errors positive  (so they don't cancel each other out)
     Error of -5 and +5 → both become 25 ✅

  2. Penalizes large errors MORE than small ones
     Error of 2 → 4
     Error of 4 → 16  (4x larger error = 4x2 = 16x bigger penalty!)

This forces the AI to care about big mistakes more than small ones.

tutorial.title: MSE Formula
tutorial.content:
predictions = [2.5, 0.0, 2.0]
actuals     = [3.0, -0.5, 2.0]

squared_errors = [(p - a)**2 for p, a in zip(predictions, actuals)]
mse = sum(squared_errors) / len(squared_errors)

print(f'Squared errors: {squared_errors}')
print(f'MSE: {mse:.4f}')

tutorial.title: Interpreting MSE
tutorial.content:
MSE = 0.0   → Perfect predictions (rarely happens!)
  MSE small   → Model is doing well
  MSE large   → Model needs more training

During training, the AI's goal is to MINIMIZE the MSE by
adjusting its weights — this is gradient descent!

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: Why do we SQUARE the errors in MSE?
options: To make errors positive and penalize large errors more
It doesn't matter which operation we use

tutorial.title: Computers Don't Read Words
tutorial.content:
Computers only understand numbers. To process text (like ChatGPT does),
we first chop it up into smaller pieces called 'tokens'.
These tokens are then converted into numbers the model can work with.

tutorial.title: Types of Tokenization
tutorial.content:
There are different ways to tokenize:

  Word-level:      'Hello world' → ['Hello', 'world']
  Character-level: 'Hello' → ['H', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o']
  Subword (GPT):   'unbelievable' → ['un', 'believ', 'able']

GPT and most modern LLMs use subword tokenization to handle
unknown words efficiently.

tutorial.title: Simple Tokenizer
tutorial.content:
def simple_tokenize(text):
    # Lowercase and strip punctuation
    clean = ''.join(c for c in text if c.isalnum() or c.isspace())
    return clean.lower().split()

sentence = 'Hello, AI World! This is Python.'
tokens = simple_tokenize(sentence)
print(tokens)

tutorial.title: Building a Vocabulary
tutorial.content:
After tokenizing, we build a vocabulary:
  {'hello': 0, 'world': 1, 'ai': 2, ...}

Then each word gets replaced by its number:
  'hello world' → [0, 1]

Now the AI can work with these numbers as vectors and matrices!

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: Why must text be converted to numbers for AI?
options: Because computers can only do math on numbers

tutorial.title: Making Decisions
tutorial.content:
Activation functions decide whether a neuron should 'fire' or not.
They introduce non-linearity, which is what allows AI to learn
complex patterns — without them, the whole network would just be
a single linear equation.

tutorial.title: ReLU — The Most Popular
tutorial.content:
ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) is the most widely used activation:

  ReLU(x) = max(0, x)

  x = -5  → 0    (neuron doesn't fire)
  x = 0   → 0    (neuron doesn't fire)
  x = 3   → 3    (neuron fires at full strength)

Why it works: Simple, fast, and avoids the 'vanishing gradient' problem.

tutorial.title: ReLU Example
Sigmoid — For Probabilities
tutorial.content:
Sigmoid maps any number to a value between 0 and 1.
This is useful for the output layer when you need a probability:

  sigmoid(x) = 1 / (1 + e^(-x))

  x = -10  → ~0.00  (very unlikely)
  x = 0    →  0.50  (50/50)
  x = 10   → ~1.00  (very likely)

Used in binary classification (spam / not spam)

tutorial.title: Try It: Sigmoid
The Learning Algorithm
tutorial.content:
Gradient descent is how AI learns. It iteratively adjusts its 'weights'
to reduce the error between its prediction and the correct answer.

Another way to think of it: finding the lowest point in a hilly landscape
by always stepping downhill.

tutorial.title: The Rolling Ball Analogy
tutorial.content:
Imagine you're blindfolded on a hilly surface and want to find the valley:

  1. Feel which direction is downhill (this is the 'gradient')
  2. Take a small step in that direction
  3. Repeat until you can't go further down

The 'learning rate' controls HOW BIG each step is:
  • Too large → overshoot the valley!
  • Too small → takes forever to reach it
  • Just right → converges to the minimum ✅

tutorial.title: Weight Update Formula
tutorial.content:
# One step of gradient descent:
# new_weight = old_weight - learning_rate * gradient

weight = 5.0
learning_rate = 0.1

for step in range(5):
    gradient = 2 * weight   # Example: gradient of w^2
    weight = weight - learning_rate * gradient
    print(f'Step {step+1}: weight = {weight:.4f}')

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: What happens if the learning rate is TOO LARGE?

tutorial.title: Categorical Data
tutorial.content:
We can't feed strings like 'cat' or 'dog' directly into a math equation.
One-Hot Encoding converts them into vectors of 0s and 1s that AI can use.

One column per category. The matching category gets a 1, all others get 0.

tutorial.title: Why Not Just Use Numbers?
tutorial.content:
You might think: why not just use cat=0, dog=1, bird=2?

The problem: the AI would assume dog(1) is 'between' cat(0) and bird(2),
or that bird is 'twice' a dog. That's meaningless!

One-hot encoding avoids this by treating each category independently:
  cat  = [1, 0, 0]
  dog  = [0, 1, 0]
  bird = [0, 0, 1]

tutorial.title: Building One-Hot Vectors
tutorial.content:
def one_hot(categories, target):
    return [1 if c == target else 0 for c in categories]

animals = ['cat', 'dog', 'bird']
print('cat: ', one_hot(animals, 'cat'))
print('dog: ', one_hot(animals, 'dog'))
print('bird:', one_hot(animals, 'bird'))

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: With categories ['red','green','blue'], what is the one-hot for 'green'?
